learning_outcomes.description: Critically analyse the relationship between urban design, social and environmental systems, and their impacts, through individual and/or collaborative analysis and propositions.
Deploy a reflexive and research-driven design process.
Integrate contemporary urban strategies, practice and discourse in the design of a complex project at the urban scale, with a focus on contextual, social and environmental system opportunities.

Communicate the technical, experiential, formal and spatial qualities of an urban design proposal across a range of scales and disciplinary contexts, to a best practice standard, using emerging techniques, governance and design strategies to demonstrate urban opportunity and social, economic and environmental enhancement.

assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Presentation, Product/ Design, Reflection
assessments.task_description:
Assessment Rationale

Assessment Task 1 (A01) establishes the strategic and conceptual foundation for the studio, preparing students to move into individual spatial design propositions (A02) grounded in a shared, evidence-based framework for healthy urban environments. It consolidates Phases 01–03 of the studio. Building on initial site exploration, analytical mapping, and research into urban wellbeing, each team develops a strategic spatial framework for a selected precinct within Brisbane’s Green Pathways / Gold Places network. 

Central to this assessment is the development and application of the Green Pathways Patternbook, a collective design resource that translates research into spatial principles and performance criteria. Teams are required to draw from, test, and spatially deploy Patternbook principles to guide their precinct-scale proposals. 

Working collaboratively, each team develops a precinct-scale strategic spatial framework that responds to a selected segment of the Green Pathways network. This framework must articulate a clear vision and a structured set of spatial, ecological, and programmatic strategies that contribute to Brisbane’s 2032 Olympic and Paralympic legacy as a city of healthy, connected places.

Phase 01 – Context Analysis: Olympics, Legacy, and Urban Systems. Teams begin by analysing the Green Pathways network at city and precinct scale.

Phase 02 – Theoretical Positioning: Designing for Urban Wellbeing. Teams develop a research-informed design position grounded in contemporary frameworks such as biophilic urbanism, water sensitive urban design (WSUD), nature-based solutions, and healthy cities. This phase translates theory into design intelligence, producing a set of Patternbook “cards.”

Phase 03 – Spatial Framework: Strategic Urban Propositions. In this phase, teams synthesise analysis and theory into a coherent strategic spatial framework for their precinct, translating Patternbook principles into spatial strategies and intended performance outcomes.

Teamwork. Participation and contribution to teamwork across Weeks 1–6 will be considered through a peer assessment process, potentially influencing individual grades. If, for whatever reason, you find that your group is not functioning effectively, please contact your Course Coordinator for support.

Complex / authentic assessment using AI and/or MT to support learning. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The School of Architecture, Design and Planning uses Blackboard and/or Turnitin for assessment submission. Turnitin is accessed through the course Blackboard site. Turnitin also checks for plagiarism or instances where the original work of others is not appropriately acknowledged. Uploaded files must contain readable text and not be rasterised. Students are advised to commence assignment uploads with sufficient amount of time (consider possible technical problems with computers, internet speed, etc). After successfully submitting an assignment through Turnitin, a ‘Submission Complete!’ screen will be displayed. It is the student’s responsibility to check assignment preview and confirm successful submission. If the ‘Submission Complete!’ screen isn’t displayed, the student should regard the submission as unsuccessful. Students should download a copy of the digital receipt as proof they have submitted the assignment. Students who are experiencing upload issues must advise the Course Coordinator immediately by email and should include screenshots and a copy of the assessment for submission. To meet professional accreditation, public engagement and quality assurance obligations, digital copies of all course assessment items must be submitted in addition to any hard copy submission requirements specified in individual Course / Studio Outlines. Any physical models should be photographed and a minimum of two photographs describing the complete and full model must be included in the digital submission.

assessments.task_description:
Assessment Rationale

Assessment Task 2 (A02) introduces Phase 04 – Spatial Design: Healthy Places for Healthy People, shifting from team-based strategic work to individual design development. A02 is a formative milestone, providing structured feedback to guide the development of Assessment Task 3 (A03). While it may not carry a standalone grade, active participation and progress in A02 are essential to successfully complete the final assessment.

Phase 4 of the studio translates strategy into experiential spatial design, focusing on the qualitative and performance dimensions of urban environments. Students develop an individual project for a selected site within the precinct-scale framed network, exploring how design can shape sensory and experiential qualities of space; support mental and physical health, inclusion, and belonging; enable community agency and everyday use; and mediate between ecology and human occupation. Individually, students develop a design concept and spatial proposal for their site, demonstrating how Patternbook principles are interpreted, adapted, and applied at the place scale. They translate strategic intentions into site-specific interventions, focusing on both spatial and experiential qualities.

A02 requires students to develop a draft of their final A03 concept design submission for formative feedback. Minimum submission requirements, which are detailed in the A02 Outline (Blackboard Ultra).

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.

assessments.assessment_title: A03: Spatial Concept Design: Healthy Places for Healthy People
assessments.due_date:
Design Project Booklet + Refined Green Pathways Patternbook submission 27/10/2026 4:00 pm

Slide Deck submission + Oral presentation 28/10/2026 9:00 am
assessments.task_description:
Assessment Rationale

Assessment Task 3 (A03) is the culmination of Phase 04 and the studio as a whole. It requires students to deliver an individual spatial concept design for a selected site within the precinct-scale framed network. Building on A02 formative feedback, individually students develop and present a site-specific concept design proposal within their allocated precinct, which must:

– Translate strategic and theoretical thinking into coherent spatial interventions

– Demonstrate experiential and sensory qualities of place

– Provide a clear performance argument, linking design decisions to wellbeing outcomes

– Contribute back to the Green Pathways Patternbook, refining the principles through design testing

While A02 supports spatial design development and feedback (formative), A03 delivers the final, concept design proposition. It ensures that students not only design places but also demonstrate—spatially and critically—how those places contribute to healthier urban futures.
